ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thịt Kho Tiêu; A0CKP (pork meat, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese braised pork dish.

serving.description: 1 serving of braised pork component (without rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thịt Kho Tiêu as a main dish component is approximately 150 g of braised pork, served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Claypork With Braised Pork And Pepper (Cơm Thịt Kho Tiêu)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010569, confidence 0.95). All per-100g core and secondary nutrient values are taken from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for braised pork belly (NDB #10006) as approximately 3.7 g/100g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a retrospective study — University of Sydney GI Database; not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Thịt Kho Tiêu (braised pork with black pepper) contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100g as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010569). Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms that pure protein-and-fat foods with negligible carbohydrate content do not elicit a measurable glycaemic response and are classified as not applicable. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is 0.0 g/100g.

hypertension.key_factor: Critically elevated sodium at 1050 mg/100g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g threshold, with minimal potassium offset (47 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Thịt Kho Tiêu due to its extremely high sodium content (1050 mg/100g), primarily from fish sauce; if consumed, portion size should be reduced to no more than 50 g and preparation should request reduced fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content from the pork component is favourable for glycaemic control, but high saturated fat (~3.7 g/100g) and pork belly variants may worsen insulin resistance over time.
type2_diabetes.guidance: The braised pork component itself poses no direct glycaemic spike due to 0 g carbohydrate per 100g; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should choose leaner pork cuts to reduce saturated fat intake, monitor total meal carbohydrate from accompanying rice, and be mindful of the high sodium content which is associated with cardiovascular comorbidities common in diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) raises blood pressure risk; saturated fat (~3.7 g/100g) and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) contribute to atherogenic lipid profiles.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Thịt Kho Tiêu due to its combination of high sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100g), all of which are established risk factors for hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and atherosclerosis; if consumed occasionally, lean pork cuts and reduced fish sauce preparation are strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (pork) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium further worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week; the high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) in this dish also exacerbates inflammatory tissue oedema, and leaner cuts with reduced fish sauce are recommended if the dish is consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thịt Kho Tiêu (Vietnamese Braised Pork with Black Pepper) is a classic Vietnamese home-style dish consisting of pork — typically pork belly or shoulder — slow-braised in a savoury-sweet caramel sauce made from fish sauce, sugar, garlic, shallots, and an assertive quantity of freshly cracked black pepper. The dish is traditionally cooked in a clay pot, which imparts a gentle, even heat and allows the sauce to reduce into a glossy, deeply flavoured coating on the pork. The texture of the pork is tender and yielding, with a rich umami depth from the fish sauce and a warming peppery heat. In Singapore's Vietnamese food scene, it is commonly served as a rice set (Cơm Thịt Kho Tiêu) with one portion of steamed white rice. Nutritionally, the pork component is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with zero carbohydrate content. However, sodium is critically elevated at 1050 mg/100g due to the fish sauce base, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant portion control and recipe modification. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Vietnamese cuisine, associated with family meals and everyday lunch and dinner contexts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010569) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for braised pork belly as the HPB record does not report this field; this is documented in notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010569); this is consistent with the dish being a pure pork and fish sauce preparation with no starchy ingredients in the pork component itself (rice is served separately). Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB. These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. Saturated_fat_g (3.7 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for braised pork belly (NDB #10006) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese braised pork preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g confirmed per HPB); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas protocol for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The dish description notes it is served as a set with 1 plain rice; nutrient values reflect the pork component only, not the full rice set. The dish is not halal due to pork content.
